claimed_contribution: The paper shows analytically and empirically that including or excluding zero brand sales observations can bias estimated price elasticities, even when data are generated by an MNL process.
It proposes two model forms for zero-sales settings: a Heckman-like selection model (M1) and a pattern-mixture model (M2) that conditions choice on in-stock scenarios.
It provides simulation and household-panel evidence and practical guidance on when structural zeros should be removed before estimation.
context: The paper studies packaged-goods brand choice using store and household scanner panel data, with empirical applications in tuna and ketchup categories. It documents missingness incidence in data from Sioux Falls, Denver, and Chicago, and estimates models on Sioux Falls household panel transactions.
design_and_data: The authors first provide an analytical and simple simulated illustration with structural zeros (brands unavailable for parts of the sample) and compare MNL estimates when all zeros are used, all zeros are dropped, or only structural zeros are dropped. They then run a larger simulation with 8 stores, 50 households per week, 100 weeks, and 4 brands across 24 missingness scenarios (8 mechanisms x 3 missingness levels), with 50 replicates per cell, comparing MNL, selection model M1, and pattern-mixture model M2. The empirical analysis uses Sioux Falls household panel data over 128 weeks and 15 stores for tuna and ketchup, estimated at SKU/day and brand/week levels with structural-zero identification and price seeding rules for nonpurchase observations. Model comparison uses BIC, CAIC, calibration/holdout partial likelihood, hit rates, and implied price elasticities.
main_findings: Zero brand sales observations are very common in these data; in Table 1, average incidence is 83.4% at the SKU level and 67.6% at the brand level for household panel data.
In the structural-zero simulation, dropping only structural zeros gives near-unbiased price estimates, while using all zeros overstates price sensitivity and dropping all zeros understates it.

Across most simulated missingness conditions and in tuna/ketchup applications, M1 and M2 outperform standard MNL in fit and holdout prediction, and MNL generally overestimates price elasticities, especially at the SKU/day level.
one_line_summary: The paper demonstrates that mishandling zero brand sales in household-panel choice estimation can seriously bias price elasticities and shows that selection- and pattern-mixture-based corrections, especially the selection model, improve estimation and prediction.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior marketing work on data-preparation bias in choice models by shifting from pruning and aggregation issues to the missingness created by zero brand sales and by testing explicit correction models for that missingness.
research_gap: The paper argues that despite high prevalence of zero brand sales in scanner and household panel data, marketing research had given little attention to how including or excluding these observations affects choice-model parameters. It addresses the limited guidance on model specification when missingness mechanisms differ (e.g., structural versus endogenous nonstructural zeros).
research_question: How does the treatment of zero brand sales observations in household panel choice estimation affect parameter recovery, especially price elasticities? Which model form is more appropriate under different missingness incidence levels and mechanisms, and how do these alternatives perform relative to standard MNL in simulations and real panel data?
